coredata.dc:title: Role of surface charge on the interaction between carbon nanodots and human serum albumin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Meng-Ying
Xiao, Chang-Qing
Xu, Zi-Qiang
Yin, Miao-Miao
Yang, Qi-Qi
Yin, Yu-Lin
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (Cdots) have aroused widespread concerns in the field of biomedical applications. In order to achieve better implications of behavior of Cdots in the biological environment, an array of spectroscopic, electrochemical and calorimetric techniques were performed to study the interaction of Cdots possessing different charges with human serum albumin (HSA) in physiological condition. Two polymer, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyetherimide (PEI), were applied to passivate the bare Cdots to achieve the Cdots with different surface charge, namely negatively charged PEG Cdots and positively charged PEI Cdots. The fluorescence of HSA was obviously quenched by both Cdots in a charge-independent behavior through a dynamic collision mechanism. Moreover, the association affinity of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots bound to HSA was very close to each other. In addition, PEG Cdots with diverse content exhibited little effects on the secondary structure of HSA while only high content of PEI Cdots induced obvious conformation perturbation of HSA. The electrostatic forces dominate the association between HSA and PEI Cdots while the association of PEG Cdots to HSA is initiated by hydrophobic and van der Waals forces. Furthermore, the results of isothermal titration calorimetry revealed that both the interaction was driven by favorable entropy and enthalpy, which confirmed that these association processes are thermodynamically spontaneous. Finally, the sites marker competitive experiment showed that the association sites of Cdots with HSA exhibit a charge dependent manner, namely PEG Cdots effectively occupy the site I of HSA while the association sites of PEI Cdots are mainly located in site II.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Surface charge
HSA
Interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energy Transfer Between HSA and Cdots
Forces Acting Between Cdots and HSA

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of different Cdots. (a) TEM images of PEG Cdots. Inset: HRTEM images of PEG Cdots. (b) TEM images of PEI Cdots. Inset: HRTEM images of PEI Cdots. (c) Absorption and FL spectra of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots. (d) XPS spectra of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots.

FL emission spectra of HSA in the presence of different concentrations of PEG Cdots (a) and PEI Cdots (b) at 298 K. Concentrations of PEG C-dots were from 0 to 165 μmol/L at an interval of 16.5 μmol/L. Concentrations of PEI C-dots were from 0 to 148 μmol·L−1 at an interval of 14.8 μmol/L. The insets were Stern-Volmer plot of the quenching of the fluorescence of HSA. c(HSA) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L.
Effects of HSA on FL spectra of PEG Cdots (c) and PEI Cdots (d). c(Cdots) = 10 μmol/L, c(HSA) = 0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 20 μmol/L.

Stern-Volmer plots of PEG Cdots (a) and PEI Cdots (b) at different temperature at pH 7.4. c(HSA) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L. The FL quenching of HSA by PEG Cdots (c) and PEI Cdots (d). The black line represents a fit using Hill equation. The error bars represent standard error of the mean (n = 3).

EIS of HSA and Cdots system. c(HSA) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L, the concentrations of PEG Cdots (a) and PEI Cdots (b) were from 0 to 4.8 μmol/L at an interval of 0.6 μmol/L. Linear relationship of the R
ct (i)/R
ct (0) versus concentration of PEG Cdots (c) and PEI Cdots (d). The error bars represent standard error of the mean (n = 3).
CD spectra of HSA incubated with different concentrations of PEG Cdots (a) and PEI Cdots (b). c(HSA) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L.
The 3D fluorescent spectra of HSA and different HSA/Cdots systems.

The FTIR spectra of HSA in the absence and presence of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots (a). c(HSA) = c(Cdots) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L. The pH-dependent Stern-Volmer plots for the HSA-PEG Cdots system (b) and the HSA-PEI Cdots system (c) c(HSA) = 2 × 10−6 mol/L. The error bars represent standard error of the mean (n = 3).

ITC titration data for the adsorption of PEG Cdots (a) and PEI Cdots (b) onto the HSA. Upper panels represent the raw data obtained from the titrations and lower panels represent the integrated heats of each peak (black dots) with a corresponding nonlinear regression plot (black line).
Thermodynamic parameters of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots adsorbed onto HSA determined by ITC in 0.01 mol/L Tris buffer (pH = 7.4) at 298 K.
Parameters of the site competitive replacement experiments. The error bars represent standard error of the mean (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Role of surface charge on the interaction between carbon nanodots and human serum albumin

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (Cdots) have aroused widespread concerns in the field of biomedical applications. In order to achieve better implications of behavior of Cdots in the biological environment, an array of spectroscopic, electrochemical and calorimetric techniques were performed to study the interaction of Cdots possessing different charges with human serum albumin (HSA) in physiological condition. Two polymer, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyetherimide (PEI), were applied to passivate the bare Cdots to achieve the Cdots with different surface charge, namely negatively charged PEG Cdots and positively charged PEI Cdots. The fluorescence of HSA was obviously quenched by both Cdots in a charge-independent behavior through a dynamic collision mechanism. Moreover, the association affinity of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots bound to HSA was very close to each other. In addition, PEG Cdots with diverse content exhibited little effects on the secondary structure of HSA while only high content of PEI Cdots induced obvious conformation perturbation of HSA. The electrostatic forces dominate the association between HSA and PEI Cdots while the association of PEG Cdots to HSA is initiated by hydrophobic and van der Waals forces. Furthermore, the results of isothermal titration calorimetry revealed that both the interaction was driven by favorable entropy and enthalpy, which confirmed that these association processes are thermodynamically spontaneous. Finally, the sites marker competitive experiment showed that the association sites of Cdots with HSA exhibit a charge dependent manner, namely PEG Cdots effectively occupy the site I of HSA while the association sites of PEI Cdots are mainly located in site II.

ce:section.ce:para: Different from traditional carbon based nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, fullerene and graphene), carbon nanodots (Cdots) possess many outstanding properties, including strong fluorescence (FL), excellent photo-stability, superior biocompatibility and outstanding water solubility [1–3]. Herein, Cdots have emerged as promising alternative for traditional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) in various fields, especially in the aspects of biomedical, such as biosensors, drug carrier, bioimaging agents, cancer therapy and gene transfections [4–6].

The function of cell relies largely on various biomacromolecule, including serum proteins, structural proteins, enzymes and DNA [7–9]. Moreover, when Cdots are introduced into the biological fluids, the surface of Cdots will be covered by a layer of corona consisted of other biological substances, especially serum proteins, then the original interactions of Cdots with biological entities will be replaced by the corona layer on the Cdots [10, 11]. Moreover, we should not overlook that the widespread application of Cdots will inevitably lead to an unintended exposure to the living system which include humanity [12]. On the other hand, several work reported that the large space between novel advances and clinical transformation is mostly caused by our incomplete understanding of the interaction between nanomaterials and biological environment, including serum proteins [13]. Due to the extensive utilization potentiality of Cdots in the biomedical fields, the thermodynamic parameters of interactions between Cdots and biomolecules is required to be interpreted first. Herein, understanding the thermodynamic information of interaction mechanism can provide several useful guidance for the security application of Cdots in the biomedical fields.

As one of the most important serum protein, the primary physiological functions of human serum albumin (HSA), are assumed to preserve the osmotic pressure balance of blood and participate in the transportation and metabolism of various drugs or even external nanomaterials [14]. Due to its unique function in the circulatory system and medicinal significance, HSA has been proved to be one of the most important model proteins in various biochemical and biophysical studies, including the interaction between nanomaterials and proteins [15, 16].

Previously, much attention was focused on the interactions between semiconductor QDs and HSA [17–19]. Most studies reported that the thermodynamic information and mechanism of interaction between QDs and HSA is correlated with their size, shape and surface chemistry (charge and hydrophobicity) of QDs [20–23]. Recently, there are several studies reporting the mechanism of interaction between small carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and HSA. Firstly, Mandal et al. studied the interaction between CNPs (4.0 nm) with serum albumin (HSA and BSA). They have found that the FL of SA was quenched via the process of formation of SA/CNPs complex. Meanwhile, unfolding and significantly changes of protein conformation is observed in the presence of CNPs. Moreover, the exothermic association of CNPs to serum albumin is favored by the alteration of enthalpy and entropy [24]. Then Liu's group performed a series of spectroscopic approaches to investigate the mechanism of interaction between Cdots and serum proteins (HSA and γ-globulins). However, the FL of the serum proteins was quenched by Cdots via collision process rather than formation of proteins/Cdots complex and Cdots didn't induce obvious perturbation on the conformation changes of serum proteins. Moreover, the hydrophobic and van de Waals forces contributed to the association between Cdots and serum proteins [25]. Recently, Huang et al. systematically investigated the thermodynamic information about interaction between Cdots and HSA. Their studies implied that Cdots could strongly bind to HSA to form proteins/Cdots complex via hydrogen bond and van der Waals forces, and the conformation of HSA was changed significantly in the presence of Cdots [26].

It is obvious that the great difference for the interaction behavior between different Cdots and proteins may stem from the fact that Cdots are prepared by different strategies which result in the different size and chemical structure (core and surface functional groups) [27]. Furthermore, the size and chemical structure play a dominant role on the practical biomedical application of Cdots. So it is quite necessary and urgently to comprehensively study the influence of size, core structure and surface functional groups of Cdots on their interaction with HSA which can offer useful guidance to the growing concerns related to the potential safety risks of Cdots in biomedical applications.

It has been reported that polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyetherimide (PEI) are usually applied as raw materials or capping ligands to synthesize and modify Cdots. As surface charge of nanomaterials have significant effects on their biological response and their interaction with biological biomacromolecule. Hence, it is essential to reveal the mechanism of molecular interaction between different charged Cdots and proteins which can reflect the biological behavior of Cdots.

In this work, the negatively charged PEG Cdots and positively charged PEI Cdots with nearly identical size are prepared and utilized to study the influence of surface charge on thermodynamic parameters, binding sites and acting forces of interactions between Cdots and HSA through a battery of spectroscopic, electrochemical and calorimetric approaches. In particular, the conformational alteration of HSA elicited by two Cdots was investigated by fluorescent and circular dichroism (CD) techniques. As far as we know, this is the first comprehensive study concerning the general perspective of surface charge on the interaction between Cdots and HSA.

PEG, PEI, sucrose and oleic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chem Reagent Co. (China). HSA, warfarin and ibuprofen were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All chemical used in this study were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. HSA was dissolved in 100 mM PBS (pH = 7.4) and then stored at 4 °C. All aqueous solution was prepared with ultra pure water (18.2 MΩ/cm, Millipore). The concentration of the HSA was calculated by the absorption at 280 nm with an extinction coefficient (ε) of 36,600 L·mol−1·cm−1. The concentration of warfarin and ibuprofen were determined by its molecular weight of 308.1 and 206.3, respectively.

The bare Cdots were synthesized according to the previous synthetic strategies [28]. Then the bare Cdots was surface passivated by attaching simple polymer (PEG and PEI). Experimental details for preparation and characterization of different Cdots are listed in supplementary materials. The molar concentration was determined through the mean molecular weight determined by MALDI-TOF-MS (Fig. S1).

Steady-state fluorescent spectra was measured by LS-55 fluorophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) equipped with circulating bath to maintain the temperature. Titrations were done manually by micro-injector. The excitation wavelength for proteins was 295 nm and emission wavelength was recorded between 300 and 430 nm. The width of the excitation and emission silt was set as 10.0 and 15.0 nm, respectively. An excitation wavelength of 295 nm was selected since no excitation of tyrosine residues can be provided, then neither emission nor energy transfer to the lone indole side [29].

Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescent spectra of different samples were measured according to the following parameters: the initial excitation wavelength was set as 200 nm with an interval of 5 nm, the range of emission wavelength was recorded between 200 and 450 nm and the number of scanning curves was 24. The other conditions were similar to the steady state fluorescent spectra assay.
The absorption spectra of different samples were measured by an UNICO 4802 UV–vis double beam spectrophotometer.

The CD spectra of different samples were recorded by CD photomultiplier at room temperature (Applied Photophysics Limited, UK). The scan range was recorded between 260 and 200 nm. Chirascan software was applied to control the instrument. The scanning rate was set as 200 nm/min and quartz cell with thickness of 0.1 cm was selected. The data were expressed as the mean residue ellipicity (MRE). The signal of PBS buffer which was measured under identical experimental conditions were applied as control and subtracted from the spectra of sample.

ITC assay was performed by an ultrasensitive MicroCal VP-ITC microcalorimeter (Malvern, USA) at constant atmospheric pressure and constant temperature (298 K). Both the HSA and Cdots were dissolved in 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4). Before each experiment, all the solution was stirred under a vacuum for at least 5 min to eliminate air bubbles. Then HSA (0.002 mmol/L) was placed into the sample cell of the calorimeter (1.44 mL) and the Cdots (1 mmol/L) were loaded into the injection syringe. During each experiment, after the first 2 μL Cdots and then 27 injections of 10 μL titrant were performed with interval times of 240 s. The reference power was set at 5 μcal/s, the stirrer speed was kept constant (307 rpm). A control experiment was conducted by a separate but identical experiment, namely injecting the C-dots solution into the buffer to exclude the heat of dilution.

The results were analyzed by the Origin 7.0 software package provided by the manufacturer. The corrected binding isotherms were fitted by least-squares regression via a one binding site model. Then the association constants (K a), enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy change (ΔS) and binding stoichiometry (n) related to the interaction can be obtained.

Firstly, the bare Cdots were prepared according to our previous work through one-step carbonization of sucrose by oleic acid [28]. Then the surface passivation was employed to functionalize Cdots with two polymers with different charges, namely PEG and PEI, via heating the mixtures under reflux for 48 h.

Both Cdots are well monodisperse and nearly spherical with a narrow distribution (Fig. 1a and b). The statistical average sizes of both Cdots were 2.5 ± 0.9 nm, which are very close to each other (Fig. S2). However, clear lattice fringes can hardly be observed in the HRTEM images of Cdots (insets of Fig. 1a and b) which implied that the Cdots were amorphous in nature. When excited by 365 nm, both Cdots exhibited an obvious FL peak around 480 nm (Fig. 1c). The FL intensity of PEI Cdots is much stronger which may associate with nitrogen functionalized onto the bare Cdots. Typical UV–vis absorption spectra were given in Fig. 1c. An obvious peak at 280 nm were observed in both Cdots, which could be assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 bond [30] .

The surface element states and functional groups were characterized by XPS and FTIR. PEG Cdots showed two peaks centered at 285.1 and 532.1 eV which indicated the presence of C and O in PEG Cdots. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra proved that PEI Cdots were consist of C, N and O (Fig. 1d). The FTIR spectra of PEG Cdots displayed several obvious peaks between 1000 and 1300 cm−1 which may associate with the stretching vibration of CO and COC. Moreover, two peaks at 1567 and 1456 cm−1 were observed in FTIR spectra of PEI Cdots which could be assigned to bending vibration of NH and CN, respectively (Fig. S3). Combined results of XPS and FTIR further confirmed that two polymers were successfully functionalized onto the bare Cdots. In addition, the zeta potential for PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots were −18.3 and 15.9 mV, suggesting that two Cdots possessed negative charge and positive charge, respectively.

The FL of HSA came from the tryptophan, which can be significantly affected by some molecules, is sensitive to its local surroundings [31, 32]. So steady-state fluorescent methods were applied to study the FL quenching efficiency and mechanism of HSA by two Cdots. The influence of Cdots with different content on the FL intensities of HSA under 298 K are shown in Fig. 2a and b. It is obvious that the FL of HSA was gradually quenched by the continuous addition of PEG Cdots while maximum FL wavelength remained constant during this process. Whereas, PEG Cdots alone showed no emission under identical conditions. Similar trends could be observed for PEI Cdots. These results suggest that PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots can effectively bind to HSA and they have little effects on the microenvironment of tryptophan residue [31].

On the other hand, the influence of serum protein on the FL change of Cdots is also studied accordingly. With the increasing content of HSA, the FL of both Cdots increase slightly which suggest that Cdots may interact with HSA (Fig. 2c and d). However, the FL of Cdots is insensitive to the local environment on their surface. So the following interactions were investigated with the help of intrinsic FL of tryptophan residue in HSA.

It is widely accepted that the FL quenching mechanism are usually divided into either static (complex formation) or dynamic (collisional encounters) which can be distinguished by the corresponding different temperature dependent behavior [33]. As for the static quenching mode, the quenching efficiency decreases with increase of temperature as the static quenching stems from the ground-state complex formations. However, as dynamic quenching derived from the collision between the Cdots and HSA, so higher temperature results in higher quenching efficiency [34].

The FL quenching behavior of HSA by Cdots can be characterized by the well-known Stern-Volmer (SV) equation [33]:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
c
where F 0 and F represents the FL intensities of HSA in the absence and presence of the Cdots, respectively. [c] is the concent of the Cdots and K sv stands for the SV quenching constant. As shown in the insets in Fig. 2a and b, it is clearly that quenching results of both PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots agree excellently with the linear relationship at low concentrations. However, the results deviate from the initial linearity at high concentrations. So the following experiments were carried out within the linear part of SV equation (PEG Cdots varied from 0 to 33 μmol/L and PEI Cdots varied from 0 to 29.6 μmol/L).

The plots of SV equation for PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots at three different temperatures are displayed in Fig. 3a and b. It is obvious that both K sv for PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots positive correlating with the temperature which indicates that the FL quenching mechanism is dynamic collision rather than ground state complex formation [22]. Furthermore, the K sv of PEG Cdots is very close to that of PEI Cdots at the same temperature which suggests both Cdots can obviously quench the FL of HSA in a surface charge independent pattern.

Moreover, UV–vis absorption spectra were applied to further verify that the probable quenching mechanism of HSA by PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots. It is widely accepted that dynamic quenching is mainly stemmed from collision which only changes the excited state of the HSA, so the UV–vis absorption spectra of the HSA remains constant while static FL quenching derived from the complex formation which would lead to the alteration of the absorption properties of the HSA [35]. As shown in Fig. S4a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of HSA and the corresponding different spectra between HSA/PEG Cdots and PEG Cdots systems overlaid very well with each other, which further confirms that the FL quenching of HSA initiated by negatively charged PEG Cdots is dynamic collision. Similar results are observed in the different absorption spectra for PEI Cdots which imply that the FL quenching of the HSA by PEI Cdots is also drove by collision (Fig. S4b). Therefore, this result further corroborates the quenching mechanism of HSA by Cdots don't dependent on the surface charge.

In order to further clarify the actual FL quenching mechanism, time resolved FL spectrometry was utilized to determine the variation of the FL lifetime of the fluorophore [20]. During the dynamic quenching, only the excited states of HSA were affected which lead to the decrease of the FL lifetime of HSA, while static quenching solely affects the ground states of the HSA and the FL lifetime remains constant [36]. The FL decay curves of HSA, HSA/PEG Cdots and HSA/PEI Cdots were given in Fig. S5 and the average lifetime are calculated by the sum value of ∑τibi products. The average FL lifetime of HSA was about 5.44 ns while that decreased to 5.22 ns and 5.21 ns after incubating with PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots, respectively. These results further confirmed that the surface charge independent quenching mechanism, namely dynamic collision mechanism.

In order to calculate the equilibrium affinity between Cdots and HSA, the Hill equation was applied to analyze the binding information of both PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots to HSA [37]:
(2)
Q
Q max
=
c
n
K D
n
+
c
n
where Q = (F 0 − F) / F 0, Q max stands for the saturated value of Q and K D is the dissociation constant between Cdots and HSA while n denoted as the Hill parameter which can be considered as a indictor of affinity “cooperativity” [7]. As for a negatively cooperative association, n < 1, when further proteins adsorb to nanomaterials, the binding constants between nanomaterials and proteins gradually decreases. As for the positive molecule reaction, n > 1, once the nanomaterials is already adsorbed by one protein molecule, the affinity for the nanomaterials adsorbed onto HSA gradually increases in a superlinear manner. Non-cooperative binding reactions have Hill coefficients of 1 which indicates that the affinity of a ligand binding to protein is not related to whether other nanomaterials are already adsorbed or not [38].

There is a great agreement by fitting (F 0 − F) / F 0 as a function of Cdots concentration ([c]) with Hill equation (Fig. 3c and d). The dissociation constant (K D) can be determined as: K D(PEG) = (7.51 ± 0.46) × 10−5 mol/L, K D(PEI) = (6.03 ± 0.43) × 10−5 mol/L. The disassociation constant (K D) can be converted into association constants (K a) through the derivation of K D: K a(PEG) = (1.34 ± 0.08) × 104 L/mol, K a(PEI) = (1.66 ± 0.12) × 104 L/mol. It is apparent that the association affinity of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots to HSA is very close to each other which further confirm that charge-independent relationship between association constants and surface charge of Cdots. Moreover, the association constants are also much smaller than the previously reported value of serum proteins binding to larger metal nanoparticles or QDs [7, 21]. Moreover, both n are larger than 1 which suggest than both Cdots bind to HSA in a positively manner. This value corroborates with our previous work [25].

When Cdots independently bind to HSA with a series of binding sites, the following double-logarithmic equation can be applied to evaluate the association constants of Cdots binding to HSA:
(3)
lg
F 0
− F
F
= lg
K a
+ n lg
c

The value of association constant (K a) can be calculated by the plot of lg(F 0 − F) / F versus lg[c] (Fig. S6). It is obvious that the K a between Cdots and HSA calculated from double-logarithmic equation (K a(PEG) = (1.65 ± 0.14) × 104 L/mol, K a(PEI) = (1.80 ± 0.19) × 104 L/mol) are very close to that calculated from Hill equation.
Besides the FL technology mentioned above, electrochemistry was also applied to obtain binding information for the adsorption of Cdots by HSA.

In the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the charge transfer resistance (R ct) can be measured via the diameter of the semi-circle in the Nyquist plot and it can be utilized to depict the interfacial properties of different surface modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, R ct values increased with the addition of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots, which indicated that the both Cdots will effectively bind to HSA modified electrodes, therefore blocking the transportation of electrons. The R ct corresponds to the different concentrations of Cdots can be determined from the Nyquist plot. Based on the changes of R ct, the K a can be determined according to the following equation [39]:
(4)
R ct
i
R ct
0
=
K a
c
+ 1
where R ct(i) and R ct(0) represents the R ct in the presence and absence of Cdots, respectively. Therefore, the K a can be determined from the linear relationship of R ct(i)/R ct(0) as a function of Cdots' content (Fig. 4c and d). The K a of PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots bond to HSA equal to (2.01 ± 0.18) × 105 L/mol, (2.90 ± 0.16) × 105 L/mol, respectively. Although these two results are close to each other which also prove the charge-independent binding behavior, these values are much higher than those obtained from the fluorescent methods. The huge difference may be caused by the fact that the association constants calculated from fluorescent techniques only reveal the interaction between Cdots and tryptophan residue of HSA in solution, namely part of the HSA rather than entire molecule [40]. However, as for the electrochemical methods, all possible binding behavior might change the R ct value of EIS, namely the interaction between Cdots and entire HSA molecule modified on the electrode surface (solid-surface interface) can be reflected through this strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy Transfer Between HSA and Cdots
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding distance (r) between Trp214 of protein (donor) and binding sites of Cdots (acceptor) can be estimated by the theory of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The efficiency of energy transfer between donor and acceptor (E) can be determined on the basis of theory of FRET [41].
(5)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
=
R 0
6
R 0
6
+
r 6
where r represents the binding distance between the donor and acceptor, and R 0 is the critical energy transfer distance at efficiency at 50%. R 0 can be determined accordingly:
(6)
R 0
6
= 8.79 ×
10
‐ 25
K 2
N
− 4
Φ J
where K 2 represents the orientation factor related to the geometry of the donor and acceptor of dipoles; N is the refracted index of the medium; Φ represents the FL quantum yield of the donor and J is the effect of the spectral overlap between the FL spectrum of the donor and absorbance spectrum of the acceptor (Fig. S7), which can be calculated according to the following expression:
(7)
J =
∫ 0 ∞
F
λ
ε
λ
λ 4
d λ
∫ 0 ∞
F
λ
d λ
In the above equation, F(λ) and ε(λ) are the FL intensity of the donor and the extinction coefficient of the acceptor at given wavelength range.

In the present case, N = 1.36 and Φ = 0.074 [42], then R 0 and r can be calculated according to Eqs. (5)–(7). As for the PEG Cdots, R 0 and r were calculated to be 1.83 and 2.53 nm, respectively, while those for PEI Cdots were calculated to be 1.72 and 2.34 nm, respectively. It is apparent that the average distance between Trp214 of HSA and binding sites of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots are close to each other. Furthermore, the r between the Trp214 and binding sites is in the range between 2 and 8 nm [43] and satisfy the required condition, namely 0.5R 0 < r < 1.5R 0 [44], indicating the occurrence of energy transfer from HSA to both Cdots with high possibility.
It has been widely accepted that conformation of HSA will be perturbed as the nanomaterials bind to them, which can be well revealed by CD and 3D fluorescent spectra.

As shown in Fig. 5 , two negative absorption peaks in the UV range (208 to 222 nm) can be found in the CD spectra of HSA which represent the α-helical structure and the n → π⁎ transfer for the polypeptide chain of the α-helical [45].

The response of the protein secondary structure to the Cdots exhibits a charge dependent manner (Fig. 5). Namely, addition of PEG Cdots doesn't induce any obvious perturbation of protein secondary structure, even at higher concentrations. However, negligible reduction of peaks is observed for PEI Cdots at low concentrations while the two bands decrease obviously as soon as the concentration of PEI Cdots further increase to 222 μmol/L, indicating that adsorption of high concentrations of PEI Cdots can induces the changes of the conformation of HSA, especially the α-helical.

The 3D fluorescent spectra reveal comprehensive and characteristic secondary structure result of the protein, so they were further applied to investigate the conformation alteration of HSA induced by Cdots more carefully. The 3D fluorescent spectra of different samples and their corresponding contour diagram are given in Figs. 6 and S8.

Four peaks can be observed in 3D fluorescent spectra of HSA and HSA/Cdots system (Fig. 6). The left and right peaks can be contributed to the first and second ordered Rarleigh scattering peak, respectively. Another two peaks in the 3D fluorescent spectra, namely peak 1 at left and peak 2 at right. As for the peak 1 (λex/em = 280/347 nm), it mainly represents the fluorescent information of the tryptophan and tyrosine residues while the peak 2 (λex/em = 235/346 nm) reflects the fluorescent information of polypeptide backbones structures and the corresponding intensities is sensitive to the disturbance of secondary structure [46]. It is obvious that FL intensity kept almost constant in the presence of PEG Cdots, which is similar to the influence of PEI Cdots with low concentrations. However, when the high concentration of PEI Cdots are added, the FL intensity has been quenched obviously (13.4% for peak 2) (Table S1), further indicating that the adsorption of PEI Cdots (high concentration) to HSA would induce a slight perturbation of polypeptide backbones of HSA which corroborates with the results obtained from CD spectra. As soon as more PEI Cdots are added, the conformation of HSA change obviously and HSA can adopt a more incompact state, which results in the more hydrophobic tryptophan residue exposed to the hydrophobic cavities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Forces Acting Between Cdots and HSA
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the physical forces related to the interaction of small molecules with biomolecules can be classified as electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic, and van der Waals, which can also be found in the interactions between nanomaterials and proteins, contributing the significant biological features of nanomaterials [7].

The FTIR spectra of HSA display two peaks centered at 1545 and 1650 cm−1, which can be contributed to the CO stretch vibration of amide I and CN stretch vibration and NH bending of amide II (Fig. 7a). It is very clear that the wavenumber of amide I and amide II remains constant in the existence of Cdots while the probability of hydrogen bonding interaction between two kinds of Cdots with HSA can be excluded [47].

Then pH dependent quenching studies were applied to study the role of electrostatic forces on the binding between Cdots and HSA. As the isoelectric point of HSA is about 4.7 [48], the pH of the buffer are selected as 4.5, 5.8 and 7.4. The influence of the pH on the SV plots for the FL quenching of HSA by two Cdots is shown in Fig. 7b and c. It is very obvious that the buffer pH has little influence on the quenching behavior of HSA by PEG Cdots while the K sv of HSA by PEI Cdots exhibit obvious pH dependent quenching behavior, namely the K sv decrease obviously with the increase of the pH. The pH dependent quenching study indicates that the electrostatic forces may not be involved in the association between PEG Cdots and HSA while that may be the main driving forces for the PEI Cdots binding to HSA. These results indicate that the two Cdots bind to HSA through a charge dependent behavior, namely the PEG Cdots associate with HSA through hydrophobic and van der Waals while PEI Cdots interact with HSA through electrostatic interaction.

Recently, ITC has become a powerful tool to get thermodynamic information of diverse molecular interaction [49]. So the thermodynamic parameters about the interaction of Cdots (PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots) with HSA are got from ITC. ITC technique can provide quantitative thermodynamic information of molecular interaction, including enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy change (ΔS), free energy change (ΔG) as well as the binding affinity (K a) and binding stoichiometry (n) of the interaction occurred in solution.

The representative calorimetric curves of titration of Cdots (PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots) into HSA solution at 298 K and pH = 7.4 was displayed in Fig. 8 (upper panels). Each peak in the calorimetric titration curves denoted as a single injection of Cdots into the HSA solution. Obviously, the titration of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots into HSA solution yield negative heat peaks, which indicating that both the association was an exothermic process.

The resulting heat values were plotted against the molar ratio of Cdots/HSA and the corresponding titration data was fitted by a standard nonlinear least-squares regression with one set of sites model (Fig. 8). The generated heat in each injection was adjusted by the heat of dilution. Then thermodynamic parameters related to the association of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots with HSA was given in Table 1 . The ΔH and ΔS related to interaction of PEG Cdots with HSA are −1.48 kJ/mol and 100.83 J/mol/K, respectively, while those for PEG Cdots bind to HSA are −7.59 kJ/mol and 57.74 J/mol/K, respectively. These results indicate that the binding of both Cdots to HSA was initiated by favorable changes of enthalpy and entropy. Furthermore, the calculated negative value of ΔG confirms that association process of PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots to HSA is thermodynamically spontaneous.

The association affinities of both Cdots to HSA are of the same order of magnitude (104 L/mol) which are also very close to that obtained from above FL titration methods. However, compared with PEG Cdots, the binding affinity between PEI Cdots and HSA determined by ITC is much larger. We speculate that this abnormal result may be caused by the association affinity between both Cdots and HSA is pretty low, while the association affinity determined by ITC cannot be reliably if the affinity is around or below 104 mol/L [50, 51]. Furthermore, the low binding affinity can confirm the FL of HSA is obviously quenched by both Cdots through a dynamic collision mechanism rather than complex formation. Finally, the number of binding sites for PEG Cdots and PEI Cdots binding to HSA are 39 and 42, respectively, which are fairly close to previous reported work about CNPs binding to HSA [24].

The competitive displacement analysis was performed using sites specific probes, (warfarin and ibuprofen) through FL quenching to confirm the actual binding sites. Many studies have reported that binding sites of warfarin located in site Ι (subdomain IIA) of HSA, and that of ibuprofen to HSA is in site II (subdomain IIIA) [52]. When sites of ligands occupied are similar to the specific site probe, then the association constants will be affected obviously.

In order to distinguish the binding sites of Cdots to the HSA, the HSA mixed with certain amount of warfarin or ibuprofen were titrated by Cdots. The association constants of Cdots to HSA were determined by double-logarithmic equation. The results imply that the K a changes from 1.61 × 104 to 1.55 × 104 and 0.94 × 104 L/mol in the presence of ibuprofen or warfarin, respectively, for HSA/PEG Cdots system. As for the PEI Cdots, the K a changes from 1.86 × 104 to 3.65 × 104 and 1.62 × 104 L/mol in the presence of ibuprofen and warfarin, respectively (Table 2 ). The primary binding site for PEG Cdots was located in site Ι of HSA which were in great agreement with other carbon based nanomaterials with negative charges [26] while PEI Cdots binding to HSA within site ΙΙ. Therefore, the binding sites of Cdots with different charge to HSA show an obvious charge dependent behavior.

In this work, we comprehensively studied the interaction between HSA and two different surface charged Cdots (negatively charged PEG Cdots and positively charged PEI Cdots) by a variety of photophysical, electrochemical and calorimetric measurements. The results demonstrate that the FL of Trp residues in HSA could be quenched by both Cdots via a charge-independent manner. The Hill equation and EIS were utilized to prove that different surface charges show little effect on the association constant between Cdots and HSA. The CD and 3D fluorescent spectra of HSA implied that PEG Cdots don't induce any perturbation of HSA secondary structure while high concentration of PEI Cdots will change the secondary structure of HSA obviously which is quite different from PEI Cdots with low concentration. The acting forces between Cdots and HSA show an obvious surface charge dependent behavior. Namely, the electrostatic forces dominate the binding reaction between HSA and PEI Cdots while PEG Cdots bind to HSA mainly through hydrophobic and van der Waals forces. The thermodynamic parameters had been calculated by the ITC experiments. Enthalpy and entropy play an essential role on the interaction between HSA and Cdots (PEG Cdots or PEI Cdots), which proved the association process of both Cdots to HSA is thermodynamically spontaneous. However, the binding sites for two Cdots were different. The primary binding sites for PEG Cdots was mainly located in site I (subdomain IIA) while that of PEI Cdots was in site II (subdomain III). These results provide valuable information about the charge effects on the mechanism of interactions between HSA and Cdots, which is important for the security application of Cdots in biological fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (21603067, 21473125, 21673166), National Special Subject of Infectious Diseases (2018ZX10301405), Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province and Open Research Fund Program of the State Key Laboratory of Virology of China (2018KF007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.06.082.
